unit.name: Encoder-decoders, T5, and the rise of decoder-only GPT models
unit.description: Introduces span-corruption pretraining for encoder-decoders, connects it to T5 and implicit retrieval, then transitions to decoder-only GPT-style models and their generative strengths.
unit.summary: For encoder-decoders, the lecture contrasts a simple split-sequence language-model objective with span corruption, where spans are masked in the encoder input and the decoder generates the missing spans marked by sentinel tokens [ts=3519s] [ts=3594s]. This objective keeps bidirectional context on the input side while preserving generative training on the output side, which makes it attractive for tasks like machine translation and led to strong results in models such as T5 [ts=3597s] [ts=3647s]. The instructor then highlights a striking downstream effect: after salient span masking and fine-tuning on some question-answer examples, T5 could answer unseen trivia questions by apparently retrieving facts stored in its parameters, though fluent answers could still be wrong [ts=3654s] [ts=3739s]. The lecture then moves to decoder-only models, which are trained with ordinary next-token language modeling and are especially natural for generation [ts=3749s] [ts=3793s]. Early GPT is presented as a successful decoder-only precursor, and GPT-2 as a much larger model whose coherent prompt continuations made generative pretraining especially visible [ts=3843s] [ts=3983s].

key_points.text: Span corruption masks spans in the input and asks the decoder to generate the missing spans with sentinel markers.
Span corruption combines bidirectional input context with generative output prediction.
T5’s salient span masking enabled some implicit retrieval of factual answers after fine-tuning for open-domain QA.
Decoder-only pretraining is standard next-token language modeling and is naturally suited to generation.
GPT can be fine-tuned on tasks such as natural language inference by concatenating inputs and predicting a label.
GPT-2 showed surprisingly coherent long-form continuation from prompts at a much larger scale than early GPT.

kp_catalog.name: Span corruption for encoder-decoders
kp_catalog.description: Pretraining an encoder-decoder by masking contiguous spans in the input and generating the missing spans on the output side.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because span corruption for encoder-decoders clarifies pretraining an encoder-decoder by masking contiguous spans in the input and generating the missing spans on the output side, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Decoder-only pretraining
kp_catalog.description: Training a causal decoder with next-token prediction, the foundation of GPT-style generative models.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because decoder-only pretraining formalizes how training a causal decoder with next-token prediction, the foundation of GPT-style generative models, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Autoregressive language modeling
kp_catalog.description: Training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly. Autoregressive models make sequence modeling tractable by factoring p(x) into conditional next-part distributions over an ordered decomposition of x.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because autoregressive language modeling formalizes how training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
